title: Listening - Part 4: Presenting the Weather
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a woman called Rachel, who presents television weather forecasts. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest today is Rachel Chen, a meteorologist who presents the morning weather on regional television. Rachel, were you interested in weather as a child? || Rachel: I became interested quite suddenly. When I was thirteen, our town experienced a powerful summer storm and our electricity went off for a day. [24]Instead of being frightened by it, I wanted to understand why the storm had developed so quickly[/24]. I began recording temperatures and cloud types in a notebook, although my early forecasts were not very accurate. || Presenter: So did you immediately aim for television? || Rachel: Absolutely not. At university I expected to work behind a computer, analysing weather patterns for organisations such as airports. Television appeared by chance: a presenter was absent and a producer asked whether I could explain a forecast on camera. [25]I was surprised to discover that I enjoyed turning complicated data into information ordinary viewers could use[/25]. That became as rewarding as the science itself. || Presenter: Does presenting look easier than it is? || Rachel: People assume we simply stand in front of a map and read temperatures. In reality, the forecast may change a few minutes before the programme. [26]The hardest skill is deciding which information matters most when there is only a short time to explain it[/26]. Viewers need practical guidance, not every detail available on our screens. || Presenter: Have you ever made a forecast that caused problems? || Rachel: Once I said showers would probably clear before an outdoor festival. The rain continued all afternoon, and people complained online. That was uncomfortable, but [27]it taught me to explain uncertainty instead of making the forecast sound more definite than the evidence allows[/27]. Now I say clearly when different outcomes remain possible. || Presenter: Do viewers often contact you? || Rachel: Yes. Some send beautiful photographs of unusual clouds. Others write when they dislike the weather, as if I caused it! The messages I value most are practical ones. [28]A farmer once told me that an early frost warning allowed him to protect a crop overnight[/28]. Knowing a forecast has helped someone act in time is extremely satisfying. || Presenter: Has technology changed the job? || Rachel: Satellite images are more detailed and computer models improve constantly. Yet technology does not remove the need for judgement. [29]Two forecasting models can show different possibilities, and a meteorologist still has to compare them carefully[/29]. Computers are powerful tools, but they do not simply deliver one perfect answer. || Presenter: What advice do you give students interested in the field? || Rachel: Study maths and science, of course, but don’t ignore communication. [30]Being able to explain an idea clearly is just as important as understanding the numbers behind it[/30], especially when weather may affect people's safety or travel plans.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What first made Rachel interested in weather?

options.A: She enjoyed learning about storms after experiencing one.
options.B: Her school required her to keep weather records.
options.C: She was frightened by reports of extreme weather.

questions.explanation: After a powerful storm, Rachel wanted to understand why it had formed so quickly.

questions.question: Why did Rachel choose to continue working on television?

options.A: It offered more secure employment than airport work.
options.B: She liked explaining scientific information to viewers.
options.C: It allowed her to avoid using complex data.

questions.explanation: She enjoyed turning complicated data into useful information for ordinary viewers.

questions.question: What does Rachel find most difficult about presenting a forecast?

options.A: Responding to criticism after the programme.
options.B: Reading maps while looking at the camera.
options.C: Selecting essential information under time pressure.

questions.explanation: She says the hardest skill is choosing what matters most in the limited time available.

questions.question: What did Rachel learn from the incorrect festival forecast?

options.A: To communicate the possibility that predictions may change.
options.B: To avoid forecasting weather for public events.
options.C: To wait until the programme begins before checking data.

questions.explanation: She learned to explain uncertainty rather than sound too definite.

questions.question: Which message from a viewer especially pleased Rachel?

options.A: A photograph of an unusual cloud formation.
options.B: A report that her warning helped save crops.
options.C: A complaint about rain during a holiday.

questions.explanation: A farmer used her frost warning to protect a crop.

questions.question: What does Rachel say about computer weather models?

options.A: They now make human judgement unnecessary.
options.B: They are mainly useful for television graphics.
options.C: They sometimes provide different results to assess.

questions.explanation: She explains that two models may show different possibilities and need comparison.

questions.question: What advice does Rachel give future meteorologists?

options.A: Develop both scientific and communication skills.
options.B: Choose television work as early as possible.
options.C: Concentrate on practical experience rather than study.

questions.explanation: Rachel says clear explanation is as important as understanding the numerical data.
